findings.title: Batch import never persists auto-created categories (syncWithServer is an empty no-op) — imported expenses lose their category on reload
findings.rootCause: runBatchImport() pushes newly-inferred categories into STATE.categories (upload.ts:417-419) and at the end calls `void syncWithServer()` (line 463) whose comment claims it 'persists the categories the import created'. But syncWithServer() was gutted: its body is now `JSON.stringify({})` — a connectivity probe that sends NOTHING (api.ts:74-81). Categories now persist ONLY via the per-row delta endpoint syncCategories() (/api/categories), which upload.ts never imports or calls. The per-row upsertExpense writes the expense with a category_id pointing at the new local category, and the server stores that category_id as a free string with no existence check (routes/expenses.py has no FROM categories validation). On the next /api/data pull, STATE.categories is overwritten with the server's set (api.ts:236), which lacks the import-created category, so e.categoryId is now dangling and HistoryTab renders `cat ? cat.icon : '💰'` (HistoryTab.tsx:297,311).

findings.title: Batch import never persists the companions it auto-adds — imported payers/splitters vanish on reload and become unselectable in the manual form
findings.rootCause: runBatchImport() calls addTripCompanion(activeTrip, who) and addTripCompanion for each split name (upload.ts:390-398), which mutates activeTrip.companions in STATE. Companions persist only as part of a trip upsert (upsertTrip → /api/trips), but the import never calls upsertTrip, and the only other persistence channel, syncWithServer(), now sends an empty body (api.ts:74-81). So the roster mutation lives in localStorage/STATE only; the next /api/data pull overwrites STATE.trips with server truth (no imported companions). HistoryTab masks this with a fallback that derives payers from expense `who` values (HistoryTab.tsx:64-74), but ManualTab's 'Who paid' select has NO such fallback (ManualTab.tsx:531-540) — it shows only activeTrip.companions, so after reload the imported payers are unselectable.

findings.title: Batch import reports 'Imported N' optimistically; rows the server rejects (amount > 1e9, dup cross-trip id, archived, stale) are silently dropped
findings.rootCause: runBatchImport increments `added` and pushes to STATE.expenses purely on client-side optimistic success, then fires `void upsertExpense(expense)` (upload.ts:446) WITHOUT awaiting or inspecting the result. The client filter only rejects non-finite/<=0/no-rate values (line 374); it has NO upper bound, while the server's validate_money caps at _MAX_MONEY = 1e9 (validators.py:162-165) and the route returns 400. Because the POST result is discarded, a 400/403/409 is invisible: the row is counted, shown, and only vanishes when /api/data next overwrites STATE.expenses. (Contrast the manual form, which DOES await upsertExpense and shows 'saveFailed' — upload does not.)

findings.title: Popular-format default split is computed against a roster that grows row-by-row, so equal-split percentages depend on import order
findings.rootCause: Inside the per-row loop, addTripCompanion(activeTrip, who) runs first (upload.ts:390-392), then the no-splits default does `const tripRoster = getTripCompanionNames(activeTrip)` and divides 100/tripRoster.length across the CURRENT roster (lines 404-408). Because the roster accumulates as rows are processed, row 1 (only Alice seen) → {Alice:100}; row 2 (Bob now seen) → {Alice:50,Bob:50}; etc. For the canonical 'I have a Tricount, let me import the whole trip' flow the roster starts empty, so early rows are systematically mis-split.

findings.title: Splitwise import hardcodes payer 'Me' and creates a literal 'Me' companion, mislabeling every imported row's payer
findings.rootCause: The splitwise branch sets `who = 'Me'` unconditionally (upload.ts:341) because the chosen Splitwise column layout [Date, Description, Category, Cost, Currency] has no payer column. addTripCompanion(activeTrip, 'Me') then plants 'Me' in the roster (line 390-392). Splitwise's real CSV export does contain per-person paid/owed columns, but this mapping ignores them, so the payer attribution is fabricated and balances are wrong whenever the user wasn't the sole payer.

findings.title: No row-count cap or concurrency control on batch import — a large file fires thousands of parallel POSTs and janks the tab
findings.rootCause: runBatchImport processes parsedRows in a synchronous forEach with no size cap (upload.ts:314), building thousands of Expense objects + mutating STATE + calling addTripCompanion per row, then firing `void upsertExpense(expense)` for every row (line 446). upsertExpense → apiFetch uses raw fetch with no queue/throttle (api/core.ts:201-235), so N rows = N concurrent POSTs launched in one tick. BatchUpload's parse also has no row cap (BatchUpload.tsx:95-97). There is no batching, chunking, or max-rows guard anywhere on this path.

findings.title: Batch import preview filters rows on the FIRST cell being truthy, silently dropping valid rows with an empty first column
findings.rootCause: The parser keeps a row only if `r.length > 0 && r[0]` (BatchUpload.tsx:97) — i.e. it requires the FIRST cell to be truthy. But which column is meaningful depends on the format/mapping: Tricount keys on column A (Title) but the amount/payer are elsewhere; a custom format may map its required fields to columns B+. A row with a blank first cell but real data in the mapped columns is discarded before runBatchImport's own per-row validation/skip-reporting runs, so it's invisibly lost rather than reported.

findings.title: Import date parser silently discards any date with a 2-digit year, importing common CSV dates as undated 'Global'
findings.rootCause: parseCellDate requires a 4-digit year token: `const yIdx = parts.findIndex(p => /^\d{4}$/.test(p)); if (yIdx === -1) return '';` (upload.ts:231-232). A 2-digit year never matches, so the function returns '' and the expense is stored with date=''. There's no attempt to expand a 2-digit year (e.g. 20YY) and no per-row signal that the date was dropped, so it looks like the dates were simply absent.

findings.title: History value-range filter is labeled '(€)' and filters on stored EUR euroValue, but the row's converted line shows the user's (non-EUR) home currency
findings.rootCause: applyHistoryFilters compares against e.euroValue (the frozen EUR value) for minVal/maxVal (helpers.ts:134-137), and the filter label is hardcoded '(€)' (HistoryTab.tsx:255). But the row's converted helper line uses formatHome(e.value, e.currency), i.e. the user's home currency via live conversion (HistoryTab.tsx:302-303). So the number the user sees and the number the filter ranges over are in different currencies for any non-EUR home.
